summary: Matt Rocklin introduces the 'into' project, a tool that efficiently migrates data between various formats including in-memory structures like numpy arrays and pandas DataFrames, and persistent formats like CSV, JSON, and SQL databases. The key innovation is using a network of optimized pairwise conversions rather than routing everything through a common intermediate format. This approach leverages expert implementations for specific format pairs, achieving order-of-magnitude performance improvements. The post argues that data format choice strongly impacts performance and accessibility, yet many users avoid migration because they don't know how.
key_points: Data format choice strongly impacts performance (10x is a good rule of thumb) and data accessibility
The into function provides a single unified API for migrating between any supported format pair, replacing scattered to_csv/from_sql methods
Using native PostgreSQL CSV loaders achieves 50,000+ records/second vs 2,000 with Python iterators - the difference between an overnight job and a cup of coffee
Expert pairwise conversions between formats like numpy.recarray and pandas.DataFrame can happen in-place without serialization
Many users don't migrate to efficient formats because they can't manage the particular migration process for their tools
money_quotes: Your choice of data format is important. It strongly impacts performance (10x is a good rule of thumb) and who can easily use and interpret your data.
Expert pairwise interactions are often an order of magnitude faster than generic solutions.
This is the difference between an overnight job and a cup of coffee.
Robust and efficient conversions between any two pairs of formats is fraught with special cases and bizarre libraries.
The bytes of data don't need to change, only the metadata surrounding them.
key_insight: A network of specialized pairwise data format conversions dramatically outperforms generic solutions that route through common intermediate formats.
